description: Figure 2 presents compressive stress-strain curves for [112]-oriented Fe-16%Al monocrystals tested at room temperature with a compression rate of 10^-4 s^-1, comparing the effects of four distinct prior heat treatments. The four curves represent: (1) as-grown material without additional heat treatment, (2) furnace cooling (FC) from 1100°C to room temperature, (3) furnace cooling from 1100°C to 600°C followed by water quenching (FC 600°C Q), and (4) water quenching from 1100°C followed by annealing at 300°C for 14 days (1100°C Q + 300°C/14d). Visual analysis reveals that the as-grown, FC, and FC 600°C Q specimens exhibit similar yield strengths around 400-430 MPa and comparable strain-hardening behaviors. In contrast, the quenched-and-annealed specimen (1100°C Q + 300°C/14d) displays markedly higher yield stress (~450-480 MPa) and elevated flow stress throughout the plastic deformation regime. These results support the conclusion that only heat treatments involving temperatures above 600°C significantly affect the mechanical behavior of Fe-Al alloys, likely due to variations in vacancy concentrations and short-range ordering kinetics.
